title: Psalm 100: Serve the Lord with Gladness
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Psalm 100_ Serve the Lord with Gladness (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt

subpoints: A. The foundation of the Psalm: God is good — do you truly believe this?
B. God's mercy (hesed) — covenant love like a stork protecting its young
C. God's faithfulness — He is not fickle; He keeps His covenant promises to all generations

outline.point: II. We Should Be a People of Exuberant Joy (Psalm 100:1-2a)
subpoints: A. 'Shout joyfully' — the spontaneous cry of people welcoming a victorious king
B. Joy is commanded for all people, not just those with exuberant personalities
C. The world should hear and see our joy — we give God good press or bad press

outline.point: III. Joy in the Lord Should Permeate Our Service (Psalm 100:2b)
subpoints: A. 'Serve the Lord with gladness' — we serve as those whose God is good
B. Serving like Puddleglum — grumpy, obligatory service dishonors God
C. Joy in service reflects the truth that we belong to a good and generous God

outline.point: IV. We Should Be a People of Submission (Psalm 100:3)
subpoints: A. Know that the Lord He is God — we are not; submission flows from this truth
B. We are His by creation, redemption, and the care of the Good Shepherd
C. Grumbling and complaining deny God's goodness and reject His sovereign hand

outline.point: V. We Should Be a People of Thankful Praise (Psalm 100:4)
subpoints: A. Enter His gates with thanksgiving and His courts with praise
B. Praise is commanded whether we feel like it or not — feelings follow obedience
C. The story of John Wesley's porter: gratitude to God for life and love, even without earthly goods

summary:
Preached around Thanksgiving, this sermon on Psalm 100 calls the congregation to be a people of exuberant joy, glad submission, and thankful praise — not because life is comfortable, but because God is good. Pastor Lyro anchors the whole Psalm in verse 5, showing that the Lord's hesed (covenant loving-kindness) and His faithfulness to all generations are the foundation of every command in the Psalm. When we truly believe God is good — even through afflictions — it becomes natural to shout joyfully, serve gladly, and sing together as those who belong to a King worth celebrating.

The sermon works through the Psalm's imperatives to expose the ways we give God bad press: by grumbling, by serving Him reluctantly like C.S. Lewis's Puddleglum, by singing half-heartedly, or by forgetting that when we gather for worship we are entering the presence of the God of the universe. The call to submission in verse 3 is not arbitrary but rooted in the fourfold reality that the Lord is our God, our Creator, our Redeemer, and our Good Shepherd. The sermon closes with the story of a poor porter who had nothing material yet overflowed with gratitude for life and the love of God — a portrait of what it looks like to be genuinely convinced of God's goodness and to live from that conviction in joyful praise.
transcript:
## Introduction

Thank you all — Thanksgiving is fresh in our minds. From everything I can tell from the people I have spoken with, most of you had enjoyable celebrations and were able to fellowship with people you love. Sometimes we worry about making sure people have company when perhaps they would rather be alone, which is also fine. But I want to turn our attention to Psalm 100, a Psalm very familiar to all of us.

I had planned this sermon ahead of time and did not know that Elder Scott Hollander was going to speak on this Psalm at our seminary chapel this past Tuesday. Whatever similarities you notice — God had plans ahead of time. We may simply be reading the same books in preparation.

As we think of our church, our homes, and our individual lives as Christians, we should remember that everything we do is a reflection of God. Our lives should reflect joy and gladness in the Lord. If not, our good God gets bad press from those who profess to be his people. When we do not reflect the joy of the Lord, when we do not act as those who serve a good God, we are giving God bad press. We are producing fake news about our God.

In 1 Peter 2:9, Peter says:

> *"But you are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, His own special people, that you may proclaim the praises of Him who called you out of darkness into His marvelous light."* (1 Peter 2:9)

That is a purpose clause. The Spirit gave us new life in Christ *so that* we may proclaim the praises of him who called us. So because the Lord is good, we who belong to him should be people of joy, submission, and praise.

## I. The Foundation: The Lord Is Good (Psalm 100:5)

We are going to start with the end of the Psalm — verse 5:

> *"For the Lord is good; His mercy is everlasting, and His truth endures to all generations."* (Psalm 100:5)

It is easy to say that God is good. We say it to each other often. But do you really believe it? Some of you have gone through very difficult trials. Some of you may be in difficult trials right now. Do you believe that God is good and that he is using these trials to work together for your good, conforming you to the image of Jesus Christ?

The Psalmist says elsewhere in Psalm 119:67-68:

> *"Before I was afflicted I went astray, but now I keep Your word. You are good, and do good; teach me Your statutes."* (Psalm 119:67-68)

And a few verses later:

> *"It is good for me that I have been afflicted, that I may learn Your statutes."* (Psalm 119:71)

Even in affliction — especially in affliction — we must submit to God, affirming his goodness by faith. Satan's strategy is this: if he can get you to doubt God's goodness, you will not trust him. And if you do not trust him, you will not obey him.

Verse 5 mentions two facets of God's goodness that frequently appear together in the Psalms: his loving-kindness (*hesed*) and his faithfulness (*emet*).

His *mercy* — his loving-kindness — is everlasting. The Hebrew word *hesed* is based on an image from the stork, which has an uncommon love and fierce protection for its young. Storks build their nests securely in high trees, carefully nurturing and shielding their offspring. The Hebrews said: God's love is like that. He nurtures us, protects us from enemies, cares for us, feeds us. His love does not depend on us but on his eternal nature, which is good.

His *faithfulness* endures to all generations. The idea of truth here is not merely that true things last forever — though that is true. It means he is true to the things he says. God is not fickle. He is not moody. He does not act one way toward us one day and differently the next. He is faithful to all his covenant promises and true to all his revealed purposes. The Psalmist affirms in Psalm 119:75-76:

> *"I know, O Lord, that Your judgments are right, and that in faithfulness You have afflicted me. Let, I pray, Your merciful kindness be for my comfort, according to Your word to Your servant."* (Psalm 119:75-76)

This is the God we belong to. This is the God we serve. This is the God who saved us through the Lord Jesus Christ. And we who belong to a God like this should be people of joy, submission, and praise.

## II. People of Joy (Psalm 100:1-2)

> *"Make a joyful shout to the Lord, all you lands! Serve the Lord with gladness; come before His presence with singing."* (Psalm 100:1-2)

We who belong to God should be people of joy. Is that your image of the Christian life? We had just returned from visiting our son and his family and our grandson. We got home at 3:40 in the morning — and I feel like I want to be grumpy. The fact that I am not here being grumpy is a sign of the grace of God. But there is no room for grumpiness in the life of the one who belongs to this good God.

The Psalm tells us four things about this joy.

### Joy Should Be Exuberant

"Make a joyful *shout* to the Lord." This word refers to the spontaneous shout of victory that greeted a king returning from battle. In ancient times, a messenger would run from the battlefield with the good news: "Our king won! He is victorious! He is marching to the city tomorrow!" The people would line the streets waiting for that glorious moment. And when the king rode through the city gate in triumph, the crowd would roar with applause and cheers. That is the shouting mentioned here.

If God has worked a miracle in your life, shout for joy. If he has answered your prayer, shout for joy. And if you believe in Jesus Christ, he has conquered for you — you are more than conquerors. Shout for joy.

And notice: the Psalm does not say, "Shout joyfully to the Lord, all you who have exuberant personalities." It says, "All you lands" — all the earth. There is a missionary dimension here. The earth must know who God is. And one way it will know he is worth shouting about is by seeing joyful Christians. Are they seeing joyful Christians when they see you? Are you giving God bad press or good press as the world observes your life?

At the end of Nehemiah 12, when the people of God gathered to worship, it says their joy was heard at a distance — not merely their singing or shouting, but their *joy*.

### Joy Should Permeate Our Service

"Serve the Lord with gladness." There are two parts to this command: serve the Lord, and do it with gladness. Do you serve the Lord? Do you do it with gladness for all that he has done for you?

People serve sports, recreation, video games. The average video game player in the United States is 35 years old and plays more than 20 hours a week — that boggles my mind. People serve movies, music, business, the stock market. How about you?

Charles Spurgeon's *Treasury of David* — his three-volume commentary on the Psalms — quotes a preacher named George Bowen, who asks: "Can you bear to be waited upon by a servant who goes moping and dejected to his every task? You would rather have no servant at all than one who evidently finds your service cheerless and irksome."

How often is that exactly how we serve the Lord? "Okay, I guess I have to go to church." As if we are serving a taskmaster who is not good and has not done great things for us. Think of Puddleglum in C.S. Lewis's *The Silver Chair* — everything is delivered with that cheerless, reluctant tone: "I suppose I'd better." We laugh, but often that is exactly how we serve God. As if he does not deserve to be served. As if he is not good to us. As if the people beside us are not our brothers and sisters.

### Joy Should Be Expressed in Singing

"Come before His presence with singing." Do not miss the first part of that command: *come before His presence.* We are gathered in God's presence right now. And if our singing is lackluster, my guess is that we have forgotten who we are singing to — the God who created the universe, the God who saved us through Jesus Christ. How we sing as a church either gives our good God good press or bad press.

There is no exception here: "All you lands" come before him with singing. If you do not like singing, you are not going to like heaven — because heaven is consistently portrayed in Scripture as worship and song. The mighty angels worship the Lord unceasingly, day and night.

## III. People of Submission (Psalm 100:3)

> *"Know that the Lord, He is God; it is He who has made us, and not we ourselves; we are His people and the sheep of His pasture."* (Psalm 100:3)

You will not find the word "submission" in these verses, but it is written all over them. Why does the Psalmist insert this verse into a Psalm about joy and thanksgiving? Because verse 3 describes a relationship of submission to God, and submission is directly related to thanksgiving. If you are grumbling and griping about your circumstances, you are not submitting to God's sovereign hand in your life. You are implying that you could do a better job than God at running things. It is not until you willingly submit to God as God that you can also say, "Thank you, Lord — you are good, and you will work this trial together for my good."

Verse 3 gives us four reasons to submit to the Lord:

1. **He is God — and we are not.** We flip this. We act as if we are God and God is not. Even when we do not understand why something is happening, we must acknowledge: "Lord, you are the only true and living God. I submit to you."

2. **He is our Creator.** The theory of evolution has gained such a large following not because of overwhelming scientific evidence, but because it removes the need for proud man to submit to God. If God created us, we are in submission to him. He created us in the beginning. He also recreated us in Jesus Christ.

3. **He is our Redeemer.** "We are His people" — bought with the blood of his Son.

4. **He is our Shepherd.** "The sheep of His pasture." This echoes Psalm 23 and John 10, where Jesus claims to be the Good Shepherd who lays down his life for his sheep, who leads them to abundant pasture. We are the sheep. We need the Shepherd.

## IV. People of Praise (Psalm 100:4)

> *"Enter into His gates with thanksgiving, and into His courts with praise. Be thankful to Him, and bless His name."* (Psalm 100:4)

We who belong to God should be people of praise. There are many places in the Bible where we are commanded to praise the Lord — which implies we are to do it whether we feel like it or not. If we never feel like praising God, something is seriously wrong. There are certainly times when we must begin praising God because he commands it, even when we do not feel like it. The Psalms offer words for exactly that — not every Psalm is "Oh, I am so happy." And yet the Psalmist is able to praise the Lord in that very state. When we praise him, the feelings will follow. And as we grow to know him better and experience his love and faithfulness more deeply, we will praise him more and more. That is part of what it means to grow in Jesus Christ.

Heaven will be filled with praise because we will see God in all his majesty and splendor. You cannot see such a glorious God without praise welling up in your heart. If praise does not well up, it may mean you are not yet seeing God as he is portrayed in the Bible.

Before his conversion, when John Wesley was a student at Oxford, he was deeply impressed by a conversation with the porter of his college — the man stationed at the door to manage comings and goings. Wesley discovered that this man had only one coat and had not eaten that day because he was so poor. Yet the man was overflowing with gratitude to God. Wesley asked him: "You thank God when you have nothing to wear, nothing to eat, and no bed to lie upon — what exactly do you thank him for?"

The porter answered: "I thank him that he has given me life and being, and a heart to love him, and a desire to serve him."

Because God is good, we who belong to him should be people of exuberant joy, glad submission, and thankful praise — like this porter, who though he had none of this world's riches, was rich in thankfulness to God for what God had done for his soul.", as we look at God's Word. I think most of you would be able to recite the Psalm from memory, but we think giving fresh in our minds, I'd like for us to consider the Psalm. Psalm 100, this is the Word of our Lord, a Psalm of thanksgiving. Make a joyful shout to the Lord, or you lens, serve the Lord with gladness. Come before His presence with singing, know that the Lord He is God, it is He who has made us, and not we ourselves. We are His people and the sheep of His pasture. Entering to His gates with thanksgiving and into His courts with praise, be thankful to Him and bless His name for the Lord, His good, His mercy is everlasting, and His truth and doers to all generations. Let us pray together. For His God, He think of your Word, He pray that He speaks to us, even as we consider this very well-known portion of your Word, He pray in Jesus' name. Amen. Please be seated.
